preamble: The transformation from a voiced retroflex nasal /ɳ/ to a voiceless retroflex stop /ʈ/ involves two primary phonetic changes: denasalization and devoicing. Denasalization is the loss of nasal airflow, resulting in an oral articulation, while devoicing is the loss of vocal fold vibration. This combined shift represents a form of fortition, where a sonorant nasal consonant becomes a less sonorous oral stop.

examples.note: In some Chinese dialects, such as Pingyao, denasalization processes have been observed where plain nasal consonants become 'post-oralized'. This can manifest as a 'poststopped/post-affricated retroflex nasal', indicating a retroflex nasal developing a voiceless oral stop release, effectively shifting towards a retroflex stop.

sources: Xu, Y., & Wang, Q. (2007). Post-oralized nasal consonants in Chinese dialects. ICPhS XVI. Saarbrücken, 6-10 August 2007, 1406.
